articles.title: ‘Every box has been opened’: London botanic gardens digitizes 7 million specimens
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01917-7As Kew Botanic Gardens completes a scan of its collections, AI tools could help in the fight against biodiversity loss.
articles.content:
Digitization is helping Madagascar’s scientists to understand and conserve their country’s biodiversity.Credit: Jeff Eden/RBG Kew

Digital specimens from one of the world’s largest collections of plant and fungi are being made available to researchers from all over the world, free of charge.

The Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew in southwest London announced on 16 June that it has completed the digitization of 7.4 million specimens. The project, which used four high-resolution cameras operated by 100 staff and 42 volunteers, cost £15 million (US$20 million) and was funded by the UK government.

On the same day, Kew also released its 2026 State of the World’s Plants and Fungi report, highlighting how digitization and artificial intelligence (AI) can transform plant and fungi science.

Kew is making its full digital collection available on its website, which will also be searchable via the Global Biodiversity Information Facility, a portal to natural-history collections around the world. Kew’s executive director of science, Alexandre Antonelli, says that the digitization project will help to democratize access to its resources by making them available to researchers worldwide.

“In this four-year project, every cupboard and every box has been opened,” says Kew botanist Sarah Phillips, who led the digitization project. Digital pictures capture not only the pressed specimens, but also labelling that contains crucial information about where, when and by whom they were collected.

While Kew and London’s Natural History Museum have been relatively early adopters of digitization, millions of botanical, mycological and zoological samples lie underused at dozens of smaller institutions around the United Kingdom. The UK government has recently kickstarted a ten-year, £155.6-million project called Distributed System of Scientific Collections UK (DISSCO-UK) to help those collections come online, too.

Research1,2 led by economist Helen Hardy, then at the Natural History Museum, has found that by digitizing natural-history collections could add up to £2 billion to the UK economy. “We are at a moment in time where digitization is more efficient and effective than before,” Hardy says.

Extinction abyss

The State of the World’s Plants and Fungi was unveiled together with 52 peer-reviewed papers published in the journals Plants People Planet and New Phytologist.

According to the report, 400,000 plant species have been scientifically described, and that there are perhaps an additional 100,000 that are yet to be discovered.

A specialist at the Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew, handles a large fungal specimen in the Fungarium’s storage area.Credit: Ines Stuart Davidson//RBG Kew

Of the ones known to science, 29,748 are classified as at risk of extinction. Fewer than 1,000 have been formally declared extinct, but the true number could be much larger, the report concludes. Fungi are sometimes described as the ‘dark matter’ of biology — there are about 205,000 classified species but there could be millions more, said Ester Gaya, Kew’s lead mycologist, at a briefing for reporters on 15 June.

In the three years between 2020 and 2023, some 18,000 new plants and fungi had been described, according to the previous State of the World’s Plants and Fungi report. But the report also revealed that the majority of these new discoveries are also at a higher level of extinction risk. “Taxonomy is now effectively in a race against extinction”, according to a Kew statement.

Among the developments highlighted in the latest report is the reversal3 of practices that had begun during the centuries of colonial rule. Whereas the countries of Europe or North America used to hold most herbarium type specimens — the ones biologists use when first scientifically describing a plant — the majority are now in the countries or regions where they were discovered, according to the report.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the completion of a major digitization project of 7.4 million plant and fungi specimens by Kew Botanic Gardens, making these resources freely accessible worldwide. This has significant positive impact by democratizing scientific knowledge, aiding biodiversity research and conservation efforts, and supporting global understanding of plant and fungi species at risk of extinction. The project is substantial in scope, well-detailed, and linked to broader initiatives and economic benefits, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news in environmental conservation and science.
articles.personality_title: Kew Gardens digitizes 7.4 million plant and fungi specimens for global research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew in London holds one of the world’s largest collections of plants and fungi. These collections have been stored in physical form for many years, making it hard for scientists everywhere to access them.

**What happened** – On 16 June 2026, Kew announced it finished scanning and digitizing 7.4 million specimens. Over four years, 100 staff and 42 volunteers used special high-resolution cameras to create detailed digital images of the specimens and their labels. The project cost £15 million and was funded by the UK government. Now, anyone can access these digital collections online for free.

**Impact** – This is important because it allows scientists worldwide to study rare and endangered plants and fungi without visiting Kew. The detailed digital records include where and when the specimens were collected, helping researchers track biodiversity and conservation needs. The project also supports efforts to document species at risk of extinction and shares knowledge more fairly with countries where the plants were found.

**What's next step** – The UK government has started a new 10-year, £155.6 million project called DISSCO-UK to digitize more natural-history collections from smaller institutions. This will expand access to millions more specimens. Researchers also plan to use artificial intelligence to analyze the digital data faster and find new insights about the natural world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Kew Gardens’ digitization of millions of plant and fungi specimens opens global access to vital information that supports biodiversity research and conservation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les jardins de Kew numérisent 7,4 millions de spécimens de plantes et champignons pour la recherche mondiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Royal Botanic Gardens de Kew à Londres possèdent l’une des plus grandes collections mondiales de plantes et de champignons. Ces collections étaient conservées sous forme physique, ce qui rendait leur accès difficile pour les scientifiques du monde entier.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 16 juin 2026, Kew a annoncé avoir terminé la numérisation de 7,4 millions de spécimens. Pendant quatre ans, 100 employés et 42 bénévoles ont utilisé des caméras haute résolution pour créer des images numériques détaillées des spécimens et de leurs étiquettes. Le projet a coûté 15 millions de livres sterling et a été financé par le gouvernement britannique. Désormais, ces collections numériques sont accessibles gratuitement en ligne.

**Impact** – Cela permet aux scientifiques du monde entier d’étudier des plantes et champignons rares et en danger sans se déplacer à Kew. Les enregistrements numériques détaillés incluent les lieux et dates de collecte, aidant à suivre la biodiversité et les besoins de conservation. Le projet soutient aussi le partage équitable des connaissances avec les pays d’origine des plantes.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement britannique a lancé un nouveau projet de 10 ans, DISSCO-UK, doté de 155,6 millions de livres, pour numériser davantage de collections d’institutions plus petites. Cela élargira l’accès à des millions d’autres spécimens. Les chercheurs prévoient aussi d’utiliser l’intelligence artificielle pour analyser plus rapidement les données numériques et découvrir de nouvelles informations sur la nature.

**Une phrase clé** – La numérisation par Kew de millions de spécimens de plantes et champignons ouvre un accès mondial à des informations essentielles pour la recherche et la conservation de la biodiversité.
articles.personality_title_es: Los jardines de Kew digitalizan 7,4 millones de especímenes de plantas y hongos para investigación global
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Royal Botanic Gardens de Kew en Londres tienen una de las colecciones más grandes del mundo de plantas y hongos. Estas colecciones estaban guardadas en forma física, lo que dificultaba su acceso para científicos de todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – El 16 de junio de 2026, Kew anunció que terminó de escanear y digitalizar 7,4 millones de especímenes. El proyecto costó 15 millones de libras y fue financiado por el gobierno del Reino Unido. Ahora, cualquiera puede acceder a estas colecciones digitales en línea de forma gratuita.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque permite a científicos de todo el mundo estudiar plantas y hongos raros y en peligro sin visitar Kew. Los registros digitales incluyen dónde y cuándo se recogieron los especímenes, ayudando a monitorear la biodiversidad y las necesidades de conservación. Esto ampliará el acceso a millones de especímenes más. Los investigadores también planean usar inteligencia artificial para analizar los datos digitales más rápido y encontrar nuevos conocimientos sobre la naturaleza.
